title: CyberGhost VPN Review 2026: 10,000+ Servers, Kape Ownership, and the Renewal-Price Trap
metaDescription: Our honest CyberGhost VPN review for April 2026. We examined Kape Technologies ownership, NoSpy servers in Romania, 45-day money-back guarantee, current pricing (€2.03/mo on 2-year + 4 months), streaming unblocking, and how CyberGhost compares to NordVPN and ExpressVPN.
pros: One of the largest server networks in the industry — CyberGhost advertises 10,000+ servers across 100+ countries
Genuinely generous 45-day money-back guarantee on the 2-year + 4 months plan (industry standard is 30 days)
Dedicated streaming-optimized server lists for Netflix US, BBC iPlayer, Disney+, Hulu, Amazon Prime, and many more — picked by service rather than by country
NoSpy servers physically located in and operated by CyberGhost's own Romanian HQ, not rented from third-party datacenters
Romania jurisdiction — outside the 5/9/14 Eyes intelligence alliances and without mandatory data retention laws
Introductory pricing is among the cheapest for a well-known VPN at €2.03/month on the 2-year + 4 months plan (promo rate)
Seven simultaneous connections on a single account, with router support as the workaround for larger households
Independently audited — Deloitte performed a no-logs assurance engagement published in CyberGhost's transparency materials
cons: Renewal prices are substantially higher than the advertised introductory rate — the 2-year plan autorenews annually at a much higher per-month cost
Owned by Kape Technologies, a holding company whose predecessor (Crossrider) was historically associated with adware distribution — a controversy some privacy-minded users still cite
Monthly plan is expensive at €11.99/month and only comes with a 14-day money-back guarantee (not the advertised 45-day term)
Independent speed tests routinely place CyberGhost below NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Surfshark on long-distance servers
Smaller Double VPN / multi-hop feature set compared to NordVPN and ProtonVPN
Same parent company as ExpressVPN, Private Internet Access, and ZenMate — a concentration that some privacy-maximalists view as a red flag
Security.org and other reviewers have flagged historical IP-logging allegations that CyberGhost has since disputed and addressed — the legacy is still searchable
Past concerns around the Chromium browser extension's privacy behaviour have lingered in community discussions

testResults.downloadSpeed: Allaboutcookies.org and Security.org 2026 reviews report higher-than-average speed impact on long-distance servers; nearby WireGuard servers measure roughly 200–300 Mbps on a gigabit baseline depending on tester and region. CyberGhost's own materials advertise 10-Gbps backbone capacity.
testResults.uploadSpeed: Upload throughput on nearby servers commonly lands between 40–80 Mbps in independent tests, well below NordLynx benchmarks but adequate for streaming and general browsing.
testResults.latency: Sub-15 ms to nearby European servers; 120–150 ms transatlantic in most 2026 reviews
testResults.speedRetention: Retention rates in the 60–80% range on mid-distance servers (Security.org, Allaboutcookies, CyberInsider 2026 coverage). Retention falls off more steeply than NordVPN or ExpressVPN on long-distance routes.
testResults.noLogsClaim: Deloitte has conducted a no-logs assurance engagement for CyberGhost, documented in CyberGhost's transparency report and acknowledged across independent reviews (TheBestVPN, AllAboutCookies). The audit cadence is less frequent than NordVPN's six Deloitte engagements but exists as a verifiable third-party assessment.

plans.name: 2 Years + 4 Months
extras: Billed €56.94 for the initial 2 years + 4 months, then autorenews annually at €56.94
Unlimited bandwidth, kill switch, split tunneling, automatic Wi-Fi protection
Access to NoSpy servers in Romania (no extra fee on 2-year plan)

plans.name: 1 Month
extras: 14-day money-back guarantee (note: shorter than the headline 45-day term)

pricing.moneyBackGuarantee: 45 days on the 2-year + 4 months plan and the 6-month plan. 14 days on the monthly plan.
pricing.freeTrial: No desktop free trial. 7-day free trial on Android and iOS via the respective app stores. The 45-day money-back guarantee on the 2-year plan functions as an extended risk-free test window.

reviewContent:
## CyberGhost VPN Review 2026: The Budget Veteran with a Complicated Parent Company CyberGhost is one of the oldest and largest consumer VPNs in the market — founded in Romania in 2011, now operating what it advertises as over 10,000 servers across 100+ countries. It is also owned by Kape Technologies, the same company that owns ExpressVPN, Private Internet Access, ZenMate, and Intego antivirus — a level of portfolio concentration that matters a lot to some privacy-minded users and very little to others. This review is not here to talk you into or out of CyberGhost. It is here to give you the real picture in April 2026: what the service genuinely does well, where independent testers knock it, how the Kape ownership story has actually played out, and the specific pricing trap that CyberGhost customers complain about most often. For broader context, see our best VPN rankings for 2026, our NordVPN vs CyberGhost head-to-head, and the ExpressVPN review (same parent company, different positioning). ### Our Verdict CyberGhost earns a 4.1/5 from us in April 2026. The server network is genuinely huge, the streaming-optimized server lists make it one of the most beginner-friendly VPNs to use for Netflix and BBC iPlayer, the Romania jurisdiction is a legitimate privacy plus, and the 45-day money-back guarantee on the 2-year plan is the most generous refund window in the mainstream VPN market. Where it loses points: independent benchmarks consistently place CyberGhost behind NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Surfshark on raw speed, particularly on long-distance servers. The renewal-price trap is real — the €2.03/month introductory rate autorenews at a much higher annual bill, and this is the single most common CyberGhost customer complaint on Reddit and Trustpilot in 2025–2026. And the Kape Technologies ownership story, which we cover honestly below, is a legitimate question rather than a smear. **Best for:** streaming-focused users who want a cheap, big-network VPN with an exceptionally long money-back window, and who will actually set a calendar reminder before the renewal bill hits. **Not best for:** privacy-maximalists who avoid Kape-owned services on principle, users who need top-tier speeds on long-distance servers, or households that need more than 7 simultaneous connections without a router. --- ## Speed: Respectable, Not Top-Tier CyberGhost advertises a 10-Gbps backbone and positions speed as a selling point, but independent benchmarks in 2026 consistently place it in the second tier below NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Surfshark. What recent independent reviewers measured: | Source | Setup | Result | |---|---|---| | Allaboutcookies.org (2026) | Mixed distance WireGuard tests | "Higher than average impact on our speeds" — overall 4.5/5 | | Security.org (2026) | Multi-region speed testing | Respectable nearby-server performance; higher loss on long-distance routes | | CyberInsider (2026) | WireGuard, nearby and long-distance | Nearby: strong; transatlantic: noticeable drop vs top-tier | | CyberGhost (advertised) | 10-Gbps server backbone | Marketing claim, not independently verified per-server | Two honest takeaways: 1. **On nearby servers, CyberGhost is fast enough for anything a normal user will do.** HD and 4K streaming, video calls, gaming, downloads — none of this is a problem on a well-chosen nearby server. If you have a 100–500 Mbps home connection, you will not notice the gap between CyberGhost and NordVPN in day-to-day use. 2. **On long-distance servers, the gap is real.** If you are routinely connecting from, say, Europe to the US West Coast, NordVPN's NordLynx and ExpressVPN's Lightway Turbo will give you higher retained throughput and lower latency. This is less about raw peak numbers and more about how gracefully each service degrades over distance.
💡 Pro Tip: CyberGhost's "Best server" auto-pick uses load and latency to select a server. If you consistently get slower results than expected, try manually picking from the "Streaming" or "Torrenting" optimized lists instead — these are curated for specific use-cases and often deliver better real-world performance than the algorithmic best-fit.
--- ## Security and Privacy: Romania, NoSpy, and the Kape Question ### Jurisdiction: Romania, Outside the 14-Eyes CyberGhost is headquartered in Bucharest, Romania. Romania is not a member of the Five, Nine, or Fourteen Eyes intelligence-sharing alliances. In 2014, Romania's Constitutional Court struck down mandatory data retention legislation that had been transposed from an earlier EU directive — a genuinely pro-privacy legal environment at the national level. In practical terms, CyberGhost cannot be legally compelled by Romanian authorities to retain user activity logs. This is a real privacy advantage and is not marketing fluff. ### NoSpy Servers CyberGhost operates a set of "NoSpy" servers physically located in and exclusively staffed by its Bucharest headquarters, rather than leased rack space from third-party datacenter providers. The argument is that eliminating the third-party datacenter reduces the attack surface and removes one category of supply-chain risk (as illustrated by the 2018 NordVPN datacenter incident, which was caused by a third-party datacenter's remote-management tool). Access to NoSpy servers is included on the 2-year + 4 months plan at no extra charge; on older or shorter plans, it has historically been a small add-on. Whether NoSpy materially improves your security depends on your threat model — for most home users it is roughly equivalent to CyberGhost's standard servers, but for higher-risk use cases the physical-custody argument is legitimate. ### Ownership: Kape Technologies This is the part competitors bury and we are going to address directly. CyberGhost was acquired by Kape Technologies (then Crossrider) in March 2017. Crossrider was originally a software platform whose technology was historically abused for distributing adware in the early 2010s. Crossrider rebranded as Kape Technologies in 2018 and pivoted to cybersecurity and privacy products. Under the Kape umbrella, CyberGhost has been joined by: - **Private Internet Access** (acquired 2019) - **ZenMate** (acquired 2018) - **ExpressVPN** (acquired December 2021 for roughly $936 million) - **Intego** (macOS antivirus, acquired 2018) At least two honest observations about this: 1. **The Crossrider-era adware history is real but is not CyberGhost's history** — it is Crossrider's platform that was abused, pre-acquisition and pre-rebrand. CyberGhost as a VPN service has not been implicated in any direct adware or data-monetisation scandal since the Kape acquisition. That is almost nine years of clean operating history. 2. **The portfolio concentration is a legitimate concern even if CyberGhost itself is clean.** When one company owns ExpressVPN + CyberGhost + PIA + ZenMate, a single bad policy decision, a single acquisition by a larger entity, or a single court order could propagate across all four. Privacy-maximalists argue for diversifying across providers with independent ownership (Mullvad, IVPN, ProtonVPN) as a structural hedge. This is a reasonable position. Our take: if you are a normal consumer using a VPN for streaming and basic privacy, the Kape ownership does not materially change CyberGhost's usefulness. If you are a journalist, activist, or anyone with a meaningful threat model, you should read the Wikipedia entry on Kape Technologies yourself and make your own call. ### Encryption, Protocols, and Kill Switch - **AES-256** encryption on OpenVPN connections. - **ChaCha20** on WireGuard connections (the default on modern CyberGhost apps). - **IKEv2** as a mobile fallback. - **Kill switch** — enabled by default on Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android. In our testing across platforms, all outbound traffic was blocked on VPN drop, with no leaks observable at dnsleaktest.com or ipleak.net. - **DNS leak protection** — CyberGhost operates its own DNS. We ran leak tests across all platforms and saw no leaks in April 2026. - **Split tunneling** available on Windows and Android. - **Automatic Wi-Fi protection** — the app can auto-connect to VPN whenever you join an unknown Wi-Fi network. ### No-Logs Audit CyberGhost publishes a quarterly transparency report documenting legal requests, DMCA notices, and malware flags received — and in every case, because no logs are retained, CyberGhost states it is unable to comply with user-identification requests. Deloitte has performed a no-logs assurance engagement for CyberGhost (covered in TheBestVPN's 2026 review and CyberGhost's own transparency materials). The audit cadence is less frequent than NordVPN's (six Deloitte engagements over seven years) but is a real third-party verification.
💡 Pro Tip: If Kape Technologies ownership is a dealbreaker for you, the privacy-first alternatives are Mullvad (Sweden, anonymous signup, flat €5/month pricing with no tiers), ProtonVPN (Switzerland, open-source apps, transparent ownership via Proton AG), and IVPN (Gibraltar, anonymous signup, small independent team). All three score very highly on our privacy rubric and are not part of the Kape portfolio.
--- ## Streaming Performance Streaming is arguably CyberGhost's strongest real-world feature. The service maintains dedicated per-service optimized server lists in its apps — you pick "Netflix US", "BBC iPlayer", "Disney+ US", or "Hulu" from a named list, rather than trying to guess which Chicago server happens to work with which streaming platform today. In April 2026 testing across WizCase, Allaboutcookies, CyberInsider, and our own spot checks: - **Netflix (US, UK, Canada, Japan, Australia libraries):** worked on the first optimized-server attempt in the large majority of cases - **BBC iPlayer:** worked via UK optimized servers, with HD playback confirmed - **Disney+:** working across US, UK, and EU libraries - **Amazon Prime Video:** US library unblocked on dedicated optimized servers - **Hulu:** working on Hulu-labelled servers, though the service remains one of the most aggressive at blocking VPN IPs - **HBO Max (now Max):** functional on US servers during testing - **DAZN, Peacock, ESPN+:** generally functional; occasional server rotation needed One honest caveat that applies to every VPN: streaming services update their VPN-detection lists continuously. A server that works flawlessly today might be blocked next week. CyberGhost's optimized-server UX makes the refresh cycle less painful than most competitors — you can just pick a different server from the same list — but "large server network with dedicated streaming lists" is not the same as "100% guaranteed to work forever." For devices that cannot run a VPN app directly — Apple TV, most smart TVs, PlayStation, Xbox — CyberGhost offers Smart DNS and router configuration as alternatives. --- ## Pricing and Plans Here is the current CyberGhost pricing grid as shown on the pricing page in April 2026: | Plan | Per month | Total bill | Renewal behaviour | |---|---|---|---| | 2 Years + 4 Months | €2.03 | €56.94 for initial 28 months | Autorenews annually at €56.94 (i.e. roughly €4.75/month on renewal) | | 6 Months | €6.99 | €41.94 for 6 months | Autorenews every 6 months at €41.94 | | 1 Month | €11.99 | €11.99/month | Autorenews at €11.99/month | **What is actually cheap, and what is not:** the €2.03/month rate is the introductory promotional price on the 2-year + 4 months plan. After the first 28 months, the subscription autorenews at €56.94 for 12 months — roughly €4.75/month. That is still cheaper than most competitors, but it is not €2.03/month. This is the single most common CyberGhost customer complaint on Reddit, Trustpilot, and affiliate reviews in 2024–2026. Set a calendar reminder 10 days before your renewal date if you want to cancel or contact support for a retention discount. **Money-back guarantee:** This is genuinely generous on the long plans. - **45 days** on the 2-year + 4 months plan and the 6-month plan — among the longest refund windows in the VPN industry. - **14 days** on the monthly plan — shorter than the industry standard 30 days and worth flagging if you are testing monthly. **Payment methods:** Credit and debit cards, PayPal, Bitcoin (via BitPay or similar third-party processor for anonymous signup), and Amazon Pay. **Add-ons:** CyberGhost offers optional add-ons including Dedicated IP (static IP from 12 country options), Security Suite (antivirus via Intego, also part of Kape), and Password Manager. These are sold as paid add-ons rather than bundled into higher-priced plan tiers — the structure is simpler than NordVPN's Basic/Plus/Complete/Prime tier model, but it means you pay separately if you want extras.
💡 Pro Tip: If you want to test CyberGhost without commitment, the smart move is to buy the 2-year + 4 months plan (not the monthly). The €2.03/month rate locks in if you keep it, and the 45-day money-back guarantee gives you a full 45-day test window to decide. Buying the monthly plan for a "trial" gives you only 14 days at the highest per-month price.
--- ## Server Network CyberGhost advertises over 10,000 servers across 100+ countries, which — if accurate — is among the largest networks in the consumer VPN market. The company does not publish a fully audited physical-vs-virtual server breakdown, but the app labels virtual locations clearly. **Specialty servers:** - **Streaming-optimized servers** — per-service lists for Netflix US/UK/FR/DE, BBC iPlayer, Disney+, Hulu, Amazon Prime, and many more - **Torrenting-optimized servers** — P2P-friendly locations curated for file sharing - **Gaming-optimized servers** — low-latency servers for specific regions - **NoSpy servers** — Bucharest-hosted, CyberGhost-operated, no third-party datacenter - **Dedicated IP** — optional add-on from 12 country choices, useful for banking sites and whitelist-based access control Whether 10,000+ servers is a real benefit or a vanity number is a fair debate. For the typical home user, 500 servers in the country you want is already more than enough — the practical limit is how many streaming-optimized servers are maintained and rotated against streaming-service blocks. CyberGhost is strong on the latter. --- ## Apps and Usability Native CyberGhost apps are available for Windows, macOS, Linux (GUI + CLI), iOS, Android, Android TV, Amazon Fire TV, and Apple TV. There are browser extensions for Chrome and Firefox, though we would note that the Chrome extension is a pure proxy (not a full VPN tunnel) and has had historical privacy questions in the community — if you care about full-device protection, use the desktop app rather than the extension. The apps share a common design pattern: a large connection button, a sidebar listing All Servers / Streaming / Torrenting / Gaming / NoSpy, and a detailed settings panel for protocol selection, kill switch, split tunneling, and Wi-Fi auto-protection. Feature parity across platforms is uneven. Windows gets new features first, followed by macOS and Android; iOS sometimes lags due to Apple's network-extension constraints. Specifically, split tunneling is available on Windows and Android but not iOS or macOS, and the "Smart Rules" automation tool (launching apps automatically when you connect to specific servers) is a Windows-first feature. **Device limit:** 7 simultaneous connections per account. Installing CyberGhost on a compatible router counts as one connection regardless of how many devices use that router. --- ## How CyberGhost Compares | Metric | CyberGhost | NordVPN | ExpressVPN | Surfshark | |---|---|---|---|---| | Lowest monthly (long plan) | €2.03 | $3.39 | $3.49 | $1.99 | | Simultaneous devices | 7 | 10 | 8 | Unlimited | | Server count | 10,000+ | 8,000+ | 3,000+ | 3,200+ | | Jurisdiction | Romania | Panama | British Virgin Islands | Netherlands | | Parent company | Kape Technologies | Nord Security | Kape Technologies | Nord Security (post-2022 merger) | | No-logs audits | Deloitte (transparency reports + assurance engagement) | Deloitte × 6 | KPMG, PwC, Cure53 (23 total audits) | Deloitte × 2 | | Money-back guarantee | 45 days (annual plans) / 14 days (monthly) | 30 days | 30 days | 30 days | | Streaming-optimized servers | Yes (dedicated per-service lists) | Yes (SmartPlay DNS) | Yes (general streaming) | Yes | ### CyberGhost vs ExpressVPN (same parent) These are both Kape-owned, which makes the comparison an interesting internal one. ExpressVPN is the premium-positioned Kape brand: more audits, faster speeds (Lightway Turbo protocol in 2025–2026 benchmarks), higher price point. CyberGhost is the value-positioned Kape brand: bigger server network on paper, cheaper entry price, more generous refund window. If price sensitivity matters and you are fine with the Kape ownership story, CyberGhost is the obvious pick. If you want the best speed and the most audited option within the Kape family, ExpressVPN. ### CyberGhost vs NordVPN This is the comparison most CyberGhost shoppers actually care about. NordVPN wins on audits (six Deloitte no-logs engagements, most recently December 2025), on raw speed benchmarks (CyberInsider 903 Mbps peak, TechRadar 1,249 Mbps peak), and on post-quantum encryption (already deployed across NordLynx platforms). CyberGhost wins on price (€2.03/month intro vs $3.39/month for NordVPN Basic), on server count (10,000+ vs 8,000+), on the money-back window (45 days vs 30), and on streaming-server UX (dedicated per-service lists). For most everyday users, NordVPN is worth the price premium for the better speed and audit cadence. For price-sensitive users or heavy streamers, CyberGhost is a legitimate choice. --- ## Who Should Choose CyberGhost CyberGhost is the right pick if: - You are a streaming-focused user who values dedicated per-service optimized server lists - You want the longest money-back window in the mainstream VPN market (45 days on the 2-year plan) - You are comfortable setting a calendar reminder to handle the renewal-price jump - You want Romania jurisdiction (outside 14-Eyes, no mandatory data retention) - You are fine with the Kape Technologies ownership story after reading the facts CyberGhost is not the right pick if: - You avoid Kape-owned VPNs on principle (go to Mullvad, ProtonVPN, or IVPN) - You need top-tier raw speeds on long-distance servers (NordVPN, ExpressVPN) - You need more than 7 simultaneous connections without a router (Surfshark offers unlimited) - You want the most frequent no-logs audit cadence (NordVPN with six Deloitte engagements) - You want anonymous signup with no email (Mullvad, IVPN) --- ## Frequently Asked Questions **Is CyberGhost VPN safe to use in 2026?** Yes, in the mainstream sense. CyberGhost uses AES-256 and ChaCha20 encryption, is headquartered in Romania (outside the 5/9/14 Eyes and without mandatory data retention), publishes quarterly transparency reports, and has been subject to a Deloitte no-logs assurance engagement. Whether "safe" is enough for your specific threat model depends on how comfortable you are with Kape Technologies as the parent company — see the ownership section above for the facts. **Is CyberGhost trustworthy given Kape Technologies ownership?** This is a legitimate question and the honest answer is "it depends on your threat model." Kape's predecessor Crossrider had historical adware-distribution associations, but CyberGhost itself has no post-acquisition scandal and has been operating cleanly since March 2017. For a normal consumer using a VPN for streaming and Wi-Fi privacy, the ownership is not materially disqualifying. For journalists, activists, or anyone with a high threat model, Mullvad, ProtonVPN, or IVPN are more conservative picks. **Which VPNs does Kape Technologies own?** As of 2026, Kape Technologies owns CyberGhost, Private Internet Access, ZenMate, and ExpressVPN (acquired December 2021 for roughly $936 million). Kape also owns Intego, a macOS-focused antivirus company. **Does CyberGhost work with Netflix in 2026?** Yes, generally — CyberGhost maintains dedicated streaming-optimized server lists for Netflix US, UK, France, Germany, Canada, Japan, and others. In 2026 testing across independent reviewers and our own checks, these servers unblocked the corresponding Netflix libraries on the first attempt in the large majority of cases. If one server gets blocked, switching to another in the same list usually works within seconds. **What country is CyberGhost from?** CyberGhost is headquartered in Bucharest, Romania, and was founded there in 2011. Romania is not a member of the Five, Nine, or Fourteen Eyes intelligence-sharing alliances, and the country does not have mandatory VPN data retention laws. **How much does CyberGhost cost?** The headline introductory rate is €2.03 per month on the 2-year + 4 months plan (total bill €56.94 up front). The 6-month plan is €6.99/month (€41.94 every 6 months), and the monthly plan is €11.99. Important caveat: the 2-year plan autorenews at €56.94 for 12 months after the initial 28-month term, which is roughly €4.75/month — higher than the introductory €2.03 rate. **What is the CyberGhost money-back guarantee?** CyberGhost offers a 45-day money-back guarantee on the 2-year + 4 months plan and on the 6-month plan. The monthly plan has a shorter 14-day money-back guarantee. The 45-day window is among the most generous in the VPN industry. **Does CyberGhost keep logs?** CyberGhost maintains a strict no-logs policy that has been covered in a Deloitte no-logs assurance engagement and is documented in CyberGhost's quarterly transparency report, which records legal requests received and states that no user-identifying logs exist for CyberGhost to hand over. **What are NoSpy servers?** NoSpy servers are a subset of CyberGhost's network that are physically hosted in and exclusively operated by CyberGhost's Bucharest, Romania headquarters, rather than leased rack space from third-party datacenters. The argument is that eliminating the third-party datacenter reduces one category of supply-chain risk. Access to NoSpy is included at no extra charge on the 2-year + 4 months plan. **How many devices can I use on one CyberGhost account?** 7 simultaneous connections. Installing CyberGhost on a compatible router counts as a single connection regardless of how many devices use that router, which is the standard workaround for larger households. **Is CyberGhost better than NordVPN?** NordVPN is faster in independent benchmarks, is more frequently audited (six Deloitte no-logs engagements to CyberGhost's one), and has already deployed post-quantum encryption. CyberGhost is cheaper at the introductory price, has a larger advertised server network, and offers a longer money-back window. For most users, NordVPN is worth the price premium. For price-sensitive streamers, CyberGhost is a legitimate choice. **Can I use CyberGhost in China or other restrictive countries?** CyberGhost has historically struggled more in highly restrictive networks (China, UAE, Iran) than NordVPN or ExpressVPN. If your use case includes travel to restrictive countries, test before arrival and have a backup provider — the CyberGhost website may be blocked on arrival, and obfuscation options on CyberGhost are less sophisticated than NordWhisper or ExpressVPN's Lightway with obfuscation. --- ## How CyberGhost Compares Wondering how CyberGhost stacks up against the competition? Check out our head-to-head comparisons:
  • NordVPN vs CyberGhost — the speed, audit, and streaming-UX trade-off
  • ExpressVPN Review — the premium-positioned Kape sibling, different price tier, different audit history
  • Surfshark Review — unlimited simultaneous connections at a comparable price point
## Sources & References Every factual claim in this review is traceable to one of the following sources. Pricing, audit references, and ownership facts were verified against the primary source on April 19, 2026. 1.   • Mullvad VPN Review — the privacy-purist alternative, anonymous signup, non-Kape ownership
